name: Multi-thread Kent
description: Conversation deliberately switches between Army, marriage, work, and back to Army. Tests multi-thread continuity, thread selection, and resume confidence.

events.content: Kent, where would you like to start today?
Let me tell you about the Army. I enlisted in 1961 right out of high school. Fort Leonard Wood for basic, then Fort Sill for artillery school.
Artillery school — what was that training like?
Lots of math. Trajectory calculations, wind adjustments. I was good at it because of farm work — calculating yields and such.
Welcome back, Kent. Last time we talked about your artillery training. Shall we continue there?
Actually, I want to talk about when I met Janice. That was at Fort Hood, 1963. USO dance. She was the prettiest girl there — a schoolteacher from Killeen.
Love at a USO dance — how did you two start talking?
She was standing by the punch bowl and I asked her to dance. She said 'You don't look like you know how to dance.' I said 'I don't, but I'm willing to learn.' We danced three songs.
Kent, last time you were telling me about meeting Janice at the USO dance. Would you like to continue?
Let me tell you about my teaching career instead. After the Army, I went to college on the GI Bill. Got my degree from Minot State. Started teaching math at Stanley High School in 1968.
From artillery math to teaching math — that's a wonderful connection. What was it like teaching at your old high school?

Strange at first. Some of my old teachers were still there. Mrs. Henderson, who taught me English, was in the teachers' lounge my first day. She said 'Well Kent Vox, I never thought I'd see you on this side of the desk.' I taught algebra and geometry for 30 years.
Welcome back, Kent. You've been telling me about your teaching career. Shall we continue there?

Actually, I keep thinking about something from the Army I never told you. When I was in Germany, there was a night exercise that went wrong. Live fire training at Grafenwoehr. A round went short and hit near our position. Nobody was hurt, thank God, but it shook me up bad. I never told Janice about it until years later.
That must have been terrifying. What happened after the incident?

They shut down training for two days while they investigated. Turned out it was a calculation error — exactly the kind of thing I was trained to prevent. That's partly why I became so careful with math. Precision matters when lives are on the line.

expected_behaviors.resume: Should reference Army/Germany incident (most recent), with awareness of teaching and marriage as dormant threads
expected_behaviors.confidence: high — four sessions, rich content, clear thread return

threads_expected.label: Military service
threads_expected.reason: Most recent session returned to Army

threads_expected.label: Career
threads_expected.reason: Teaching discussed in session 3, not continued

threads_expected.label: Marriage & family
threads_expected.reason: Janice discussed in session 2, not continued

expected_behaviors.thread_selection: Military service should be selected (most recent active thread with open loop about the incident)
